domain:
A Person has a name and an identifier. Every Person is the child of another Person and can have an indeterminate number of children (however, there will be one Person who is not the child of any other Person). A Person has an indeterminate number of ancestors (the ancestors of a Person are their father and the ancestors of their father). We will be interested in knowing the degree of each ancestor of a Person (1 for the father, 2 for the father's father, etc.).

A Resource has a name. There are two types of Resources, such as Files and Folders. Every resource belongs to a Folder and cannot exist outside of a Folder (however, there will be one Folder that does not follow that rule, the root Folder does not belong to any other Folder). Each Folder contains an indeterminate number of Resources.

A User is a Person who, in addition to a name and an identifier, also has a password to access an indeterminate number of Resources, and each Resource can be accessed by an indeterminate number of Users. When a User can access a resource, we will be interested in knowing if they can view it and if they can edit it.

questions.question:
We know that each person has an ancestor relation. When we think about ancestor relation, is it just a simple piece of information, like a line of text? 
Or is ancestor relation made up of different parts that might be important?
For example:
1. For each ancestor relation, the degree information is saved separately, so users can filter people by degree.
2. The ancestor relation is a single piece of text. This works well when we just need to show the ancestor relation.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: We need the ancestor relation details separately.
questions.option_2: Option 2: A single line of text for ancestor relation is enough.
questions.selected: Option 1: We need the ancestor relation details separately.

questions.question:
In the context of a ancestor relation, is it always association with at least one person, or can it exist without any?
For example:
1. An ancestor relation can exist without any person.
2. An ancestor relation must have at least one person.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: An ancestor relation can exist without any person.
questions.option_2: Option 2: An ancestor relation must have at least one person.
questions.selected: Option 2: An ancestor relation must have at least one person.

questions.question:
In the context of an ancestor relation, can there be more than one person involved, or is it always associated with just one? 
For example:
1. An ancestor relation is linked to a single person.
2. An ancestor relation is linked to multiple people.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Each ancestor relation is always linked to a single person.
questions.option_2: Option 2: An ancestor relation can be linked to multiple people.
questions.selected: Option 2: An ancestor relation can be linked to multiple people.

questions.question:
In the context of a person, is it always associated with at least one ancestor relation, or can it exist without any?
For example:
1. A person can exist without any ancestor relation.
2. A person must have at least one ancestor relation.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: A person can exist without any ancestor relation.
questions.option_2: Option 2: A person must have at least one ancestor relation.
questions.selected: Option 1: A person can exist without any ancestor relation.

questions.question:

For example:
1. A person is linked to a single ancestor relation.
2. A person is linked to multiple ancestor relations.

questions.option_1: Option 1: Each person is always linked to a single ancestor relation.
questions.option_2: Option 2: A person can be linked to multiple ancestor relations.
questions.selected: Option 2: A person can be linked to multiple ancestor relations.

questions.question:

questions.option_1: Option 1: A person can exist without any ancestor relation.

questions.selected: Option 1: A person can exist without any ancestor relation.

questions.question:
When thinking about resource accesses, how should we represent a view? 

Does being a view involve additional information that a regular resource access does not have, 
or is it enough to label it as a view?

For example:
1. A view is a separate kind of resource access, because it may have additional information.
2. Resource accesses include a status to indicate whether it is a view or not.

Which option best reflects the situation?
questions.option_1: Option 1: A views are a special kind of resource access.
questions.option_2: Option 2: A views are similar to other resource accesses.
questions.selected: Option 2: A views are similar to other resource accesses.
questions.llm_raw_response:
Option 2: A views are similar to other resource accesses.

The domain says when a User can access a resource we are interested in knowing if they can view it and if they can edit it, which implies "view" is an attribute/status of a resource access rather than a distinct kind of access.

questions.question:
When thinking about resource accesses, how should we represent an edit? 

Does being an edit involve additional information that a regular resource access does not have, 
or is it enough to label it as an edit?

For example:
1. An edit is a separate kind of resource access, because it may have additional information.
2. Resource accesses include a status to indicate whether it is an edit or not.

Which option best reflects the situation?
questions.option_1: Option 1: An edits are a special kind of resource access.
questions.option_2: Option 2: An edits are similar to other resource accesses.
questions.selected: Option 2: An edits are similar to other resource accesses.
questions.llm_raw_response:
Option 2: An edits are similar to other resource accesses.

The domain states we are interested in knowing if a User can view a resource and if they can edit it, implying edit is an attribute/flag of a resource access rather than a separate kind of access.

questions.question:
Does a folder always require a folder to exist. 
If a folder is removed, would all the associated folders also need to be removed?

For example:
1. A folder belongs to a folder and cannot exist on its own.
2. A folder is linked to a folder but can exist independently of it.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: A folder always belongs to a folder.
questions.option_2: Option 2: A folder is linked to folder but can exist independently.
questions.selected: Option 1: A folder always belongs to a folder.

questions.question:
Does a file always require a folder to exist. 
If a folder is removed, would all the associated files also need to be removed?

For example:
1. A file belongs to a folder and cannot exist on its own.
2. A file is linked to a folder but can exist independently of it.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: A file always belongs to a folder.
questions.option_2: Option 2: A file is linked to folder but can exist independently.
questions.selected: Option 1: A file always belongs to a folder.

questions.question:

For example:

2. A file must have at least one folder.

questions.option_1: Option 1: A file can exist without any folder.
questions.option_2: Option 2: A file must have at least one folder.
questions.selected: Option 2: A file must have at least one folder.

questions.question:
The concept resource access potentially represents a link between users and resources. How should we understand this connection?

For example:
1. A resource access is a concept and a link that connects a user and a resource. 
2. A resource access is a concept, but it does not represent a link between a user and a resource.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: The resource access is a concept and a link between a user and a resource.
questions.option_2: Option 2: The resource access does not represent a link between a user and a resource.
questions.selected: Option 1: The resource access is a concept and a link between a user and a resource.
